metadata.title: Risk and Return in Investing
metadata.subtitle: Understanding the Fundamental Tradeoff That Drives All Investment Decisions

content.title: Risk and Return in Investing
content.subtitle: Understanding the Fundamental Tradeoff That Drives All Investment Decisions

content.label: The $420,000 Question
content.question:
You're 22 Years Old - Investing for retirement at age 65 Investment A: Steady 3% annual returns, virtually no risk, your money will absolutely be there. $10,000 over 40 years = $32,620

The difference: $419,973

slides.notes:
DISCUSSION PROMPTS:
THE CHALLENGE (5 min):
• Read Marcus's biotech story. Emphasize: 74% loss from concentration, not market decline.
• Discussion: "What's the difference between investing and gambling?"
• Key insight: Single-stock risk can wipe out years of savings in one day.

objectives.description: the risk-return relationship—why higher returns require higher risk
different types of risk—systematic vs. unsystematic, and what you can control
and interpret risk metrics—standard deviation, beta, and Sharpe ratio
personal risk tolerance—matching investments to your situation
age-appropriate strategies—balancing risk and return for your goals

terms.definition: The possibility that actual returns differ from expected returns—including potential loss of some or all of your investment
terms.example: Stocks losing 30% in one year despite long-term average of 10%

terms.definition: Gain or loss on an investment expressed as percentage—includes price appreciation plus income (dividends/interest)
terms.example: Stock rises from $100 to $110 plus $5 dividend = 15% return

terms.definition: The principle that higher potential returns require accepting higher risk. You can't have both high returns AND high safety.
terms.example: 10% stock returns vs. 2% savings account returns

terms.definition: How much an investment's price fluctuates over time. High volatility = dramatic swings. Low volatility = stable, predictable.
terms.example: Stock moving between -20% and +30% each year vs. bond steady at 4%

terms.definition: Market-wide risk affecting all investments—recession, inflation, interest rates. Cannot be eliminated through diversification.
Company-specific risk—management, product recalls, lawsuits. CAN be eliminated through diversification.
terms.example: One company's CEO resigning—barely affects diversified portfolio

terms.definition: Statistical measure showing how much returns typically vary from the average. Higher = more volatile = riskier.
terms.example: Fund with 20% standard deviation swings more than one with 5%

terms.definition: Measures volatility relative to the market. Market = 1.0. Above 1.0 = more volatile. Below 1.0 = less volatile.
terms.example: Stock with beta of 1.3 moves 30% more than market

terms.definition: Risk-adjusted return measure. Shows excess return per unit of risk. Higher = better compensation for risk taken.
terms.example: Fund with Sharpe 0.8 is better than one with 0.5

terms.definition: Your ability and willingness to endure volatility. Influenced by time horizon, financial situation, and emotional temperament.
terms.example: Can you hold steady through 30% market drop?

layoutData.title: Higher potential returns require accepting higher risk. This is economic law.
layoutData.keyPoint: 🚨 Red Flag Alert: 'Guaranteed high returns with no risk' = FRAUD. The risk-return tradeoff is economic law that cannot be broken.

rightColumn.title: Unsystematic Risk (Company Risk)
items: Key: Owning 30-40 different stocks eliminates most unsystematic risk—one company's problems barely affect you.

rightColumn.title: Stock B
items: Key Point: Same average return, different risk! Both average 10%, but Stock B is far more volatile. Higher standard deviation = more unpredictable = riskier.

leftColumn.title: Beta: Relative Volatility
items: Measures movement relative to the market
Example: Market rises 10%, stock with beta 1.3 rises ~13%. But if market falls 10%, it falls ~13%.

rightColumn.title: Sharpe Ratio: Risk-Adjusted Return
items: Higher Sharpe = more return per unit of risk = better deal
Why Sharpe Matters: Sometimes a 10% return fund with low volatility has a BETTER Sharpe ratio than a 15% return fund with extreme volatility. It's getting more return per unit of risk.

layoutData.title: Three Factors That Determine Your Risk Tolerance
bulletPoints: ⏰ Time Horizon (Most important factor!): 40 years to retirement? → Take more risk. 2 years to goal? → Minimize risk. Stocks never negative over 20 years historically.
💰 Financial Situation: ✓ Stable income, emergency fund, low debt → More risk OK. ✗ Paycheck-to-paycheck → Minimize risk.
🧠 Emotional Temperament (Be honest with yourself!): Can you watch 20% drop without panic-selling? Do you check balances daily and stress? Have you made emotional decisions before?
layoutData.keyPoint: Critical Insight: A conservative portfolio you maintain is better than an aggressive portfolio you abandon at the worst moment. Match investments to your ACTUAL risk tolerance, not theoretical.

infoCard.title: Why younger = more aggressive?
items: Stocks are volatile short-term but have never had negative returns over any 20-year period historically.
Time allows volatility to smooth out.

slides.notes:
COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS:

1. "Higher risk always means higher returns."
   → Higher risk means higher POTENTIAL returns, not guaranteed. You can take high risk and still lose money. The relationship is about probability, not certainty.

2. "I can eliminate all risk through diversification."
   → Diversification only eliminates unsystematic (company-specific) risk. Systematic (market) risk affects all investments and cannot be diversified away.

3. "Young people should take maximum risk."
   → Time horizon allows more risk, but only up to what you can psychologically handle. If volatility causes you to panic-sell, you shouldn't take that risk.

scenario.name: Emma's Investment Choice
paragraphs: Emma, 24, just started first job earning $52,000. Has $3,000 emergency fund. Contributing $300/month to 401(k).

outcomes.label: Her Choice: Aggressive
outcomes.detail: 40-year horizon justifies accepting volatility. Committed to never checking during downturns and continuing contributions regardless of market. Lesson: Time horizon should drive your risk level

scenario.name: Marcus's High-Risk Gamble
paragraphs: Marcus, 28, had $18,000 in diversified index fund. Heard about 'hot' biotech stock. Moved ENTIRE portfolio into single stock at $85/share.
Two months later: stock climbed to $110. 29% gain! $23,118! Marcus was thrilled, planning how to spend his 'guaranteed' profits...
Company announced: primary drug candidate failed clinical trials. Stock crashed to $22 in one day.

outcomes.label: The Lesson
outcomes.detail: In diversified index fund: one company failing = barely noticeable. In single stock: catastrophic. Marcus learned why diversification matters. Lesson: APPROPRIATE risk is different from gambling

scenario.name: Jasmine Compares Two Funds
paragraphs: Fund A Reality: Best year: +45%, Worst year: -38%. Wild swings cause panic-selling.
Fund B Reality: Best year: +25%, Worst year: -18%. Consistent performance, easier to hold.

outcomes.label: Her Choice: Fund B
outcomes.detail: Better Sharpe ratio = more return per unit of risk. Matches her moderate risk tolerance. She'll actually stay invested through volatility. The theoretically 'best' investment is worthless if volatility causes you to panic-sell. Lesson: Know your actual risk tolerance, not theoretical

content.question:
Scenario A - Tyler (23): Just got first job. Has $5,000 to invest, won't need it for 35+ years. Comfortable with volatility. What allocation would you recommend?

Scenario B - The Smiths (58): Both 58, have $400,000 saved. Planning to retire at 63. Nervous about market drops. How should they balance growth vs. safety?

Scenario C - Maria (30): Has $20,000 she needs for house down payment in 2 years. Wants to 'grow' the money before buying. What's the risk in her plan?

Remember: 'The best investment strategy is one you'll actually stick with through all market conditions.'

The Honest Risk Tolerance Test

Imagine this scenario: You invest $1,000 today. Six months later, due to market decline, it's worth $700. Headlines scream: 'Market Crash! Sell Now!' Friends are selling. You check your balance every day, feeling sick...

Honestly, what would you do?

A) Panic and sell to stop the losses
B) Feel anxious but hold steady
C) Stay calm—volatility is normal
D) Invest MORE—assets are 'on sale'

Critical Insight: If your answer is A, you need a more conservative portfolio. Panic-selling at the bottom is the most wealth-destroying behavior in investing. Your honest answer reveals your true risk tolerance.

questions.question: Which type of risk can be eliminated through diversification? (Answer: C - Unsystematic risk)
A stock with beta of 1.3 means: (Answer: B - It moves 30% more than the market)
Which investor should take MORE investment risk? (Answer: B - Someone investing for 40 years)
What does a higher Sharpe ratio indicate? (Answer: C - Better risk-adjusted returns)

slides.notes:
ANSWER KEY:

1. B (Systematic risk)

2. Marcus exposed himself to massive unsystematic risk (company-specific). A drug trial failure is unsystematic—it only affects that company. With a diversified portfolio, one company's failure barely registers. Marcus could have eliminated this risk through diversification but chose concentration instead.

4. Jasmine chose Fund B because: (1) Better Sharpe means more return per unit of risk. (2) Fund A's volatility might cause panic-selling during downturns. (3) A conservative fund you stick with beats an aggressive fund you abandon. (4) Extreme volatility makes staying invested psychologically harder.

takeaways.title: Risk and return are linked
takeaways.description: higher potential returns require accepting higher risk. No exceptions.

takeaways.title: Two types of risk
takeaways.description: systematic (market-wide, can't avoid) and unsystematic (company-specific, diversify away)

takeaways.title: Measure risk objectively
takeaways.description: standard deviation shows volatility, beta shows market sensitivity, Sharpe shows risk-adjusted returns

takeaways.title: Time horizon is critical
takeaways.description: longer horizons allow more risk. Stocks have never lost over any 20-year period.

takeaways.title: Know your true risk tolerance
takeaways.description: what you can actually handle emotionally, not theoretically

takeaways.title: Age-appropriate strategy wins
takeaways.description: aggressive at 25, conservative at 65. Adjust gradually.

main.title: Build Age-Appropriate Portfolios
main.description: Analyze six investment options and create three portfolios for different life stages

steps: Six Investment Options: A: High-yield savings (2%, SD 0.5%), B: Short-term bonds (3.5%, SD 3%), C: Balanced fund (7%, SD 10%), D: Large-cap index (10%, SD 18%), E: Small-cap growth (13%, SD 28%), F: Emerging markets (15%, SD 35%)
Build Three Portfolios: Profile 1: Age 60 (retiring in 5 years), Profile 2: Age 40 (25 years to retirement), Profile 3: Age 25 (40 years to retirement)
Analyze Each Portfolio: Expected return (weighted average), Portfolio standard deviation, Portfolio beta, Sharpe ratio, Projected growth of $10,000, Best/worst case scenarios
Key Learning: See how adding low-risk investments reduces volatility BUT also reduces returns. Find your balance!

content.tagline: "The goal isn't to eliminate risk—it's to understand it, measure it, and manage it appropriately for YOUR situation."
